title: GSD-intel-style atlas for Astrolabe reader
body:
# GSD-intel-style atlas for Astrolabe reader

## Goal

Port the GSD `/gsd-intel` pattern into Astrolabe: maintain a queryable project-shape artifact (structural + interpretive intel) that downstream planning stages consume instead of re-scanning the codebase from scratch on every commission. Outcome is (a) an intel schema tailored to what Astrolabe's reader/analyst/spec-writer actually need, (b) a build/refresh mechanism split between cheap non-LLM tooling (structural) and a one-time LLM mapper pass (interpretive), with freshness tracking, and (c) a reader/MRA prompt shape that consumes the intel as preamble or as MCP-tool-backed queries — turning the reader from a 25-turn exploration into a single-shot or near-single-shot pass over prepared context.

## Status

active — 2026-04-14. Pitch captured; GSD source dive complete (`experiments/data/2026-04-14-gsd-intel-teardown.md`); three-axes framing settled (storage shape × access shape × production shape); implementation work decomposed into three layer-subquests ordered 1 → 2 → 3. Parent-quest role is now scaffolding and cross-cutting design rather than direct implementation — each layer has its own subquest carrying the detail.

## Implementation plan — three layers, in order

The design work has consolidated around a three-layer architecture. Each layer has its own subquest. Implementation proceeds strictly in order: ship layer 1, measure it, then ship layer 2, then ship layer 3 (with its design work running in parallel with 1+2 because of multi-rig coupling).

1. **Layer 1 — Prose preamble generated by LLM mapper** → `w-mnz8kj86-141e5b12bf7c`
   - Turn-killer. Prose markdown docs built by an LLM mapper rig and injected as preamble into planning stages, selected per commission by a brief-type → document-subset map.
   - This is the bet that directly addresses the SSR failure: single-shot fails without prep, so we build the prep once and amortize it.
   - Ships first because it's the highest-leverage single change and proves or disproves the core hypothesis (prep-then-single-shot) cheaply.
2. **Layer 2 — Deterministic repository facts** → `w-mnz8kqb9-266e6bb9f95c`
   - Precision layer. Structured data built by non-LLM tooling (tree-sitter, custom walkers over framework metadata, git plumbing) — symbol index, import graph, test↔source map, public-API surfaces, churn data.
   - Consumed two ways: (a) cheap MCP tools for on-demand fact-checks, (b) structured snippets injected into layer 1's preamble when a commission needs exact-name precision alongside the prose.
   - Ships second because its value is bounded without layer 1 and its cost story depends on piggy-backing on layer 1's preamble-cache story.
3. **Layer 3 — Combatting staleness** → `w-mnz8kxki-bbe3be09d408`
   - Freshness and refresh scheduling. Git-SHA-based drift detection, per-layer refresh policies (cheap for layer 2, expensive for layer 1), and — crucially — the design where atlas freshness becomes a **declared engine precondition** that the multi-rig scheduler satisfies by auto-prepending a mapper rig.
   - Ships third (layers 1 and 2 have to exist to be worth keeping fresh) but *design* work starts early and in parallel because it couples tightly into the multi-rig quest cluster (`w-mnsxawnd`, `w-mnsxbn8m`) — decisions landing there could foreclose layer 3's options.

## Next Steps

1. **Await parent-quest A/B quality comparison.** Layer 1's consumer is conditional on whether MRA or baseline wins on quality (`w-mnt3t5h8` gate). Layer shape doesn't change; only the rig-template wiring does.
2. **Layer 1 kickoff** once the gate clears. Settle the document set, draft the brief-type → subset map, decide on the retrieval-layer question (BM25 vs vector vs static map only; see layer 1's open questions), spike one mapper rig end-to-end.
3. **Layer 2 design in parallel with layer 1 implementation.** Start enumerating fact types and evaluating tool candidates (tree-sitter vs custom framework-metadata walker vs hybrid). Spike one fact type (exports-per-file is the leading candidate) before committing to the whole set.
4. **Layer 3 design in parallel with layer 1+2**, specifically the engine-precondition coupling. Engage with `w-mnsxawnd` (rig template generation) and `w-mnsxbn8m` (workspace persistence) — either raise the atlas-freshness use case *in* those quests, or stage a coordination pass where their decisions inform layer 3's implementation.
5. **Parent-quest review gate** after layers 1 and 2 have shipped: re-measure reader cost/turn count. Decide whether layer 3's engine-precondition work is still warranted or whether the cheaper manual-refresh story is enough for current volumes.
6. **Retire this parent quest** once all three layers have shipped and the atlas is a routine part of Astrolabe's planning flow. The parent quest's role is scaffolding while the three subquests are live; once they close, this one closes too.

## Context

### Why this quest exists

The parent quest (`w-mnt3t5h8`) profiled Astrolabe in 2026-04-10 and found:

- **Reader is 65% of Astrolabe's spend** ($69.75 / $107.35 across 51 sessions).
- **Reader cost is dominated by turn count, not load size** — ~25 turns/session, re-reading a ~107k-token cache each turn. The model is grinding through filesystem exploration to produce a bounded, structured output.
- **Reader's output is largely unused downstream.** Analyst and spec-writer load another ~1.3M cache-read of their own material, strongly suggesting they re-read source directly rather than consuming reader's structured doc. Shared-conversation savings were only 27% — much less than expected.
- **Reader's deliverable is well-bounded**: brief summary, primary files to modify, key findings, test files, adjacent patterns, doc/code discrepancies. The shape is consistent across sessions.

The 2026-04-14 A/B batch tested SSR (single-shot reader) and MRA (merged reader+analyst) against baseline. **SSR failed** — it's still multi-turn in practice (3.25M cache-read on MCP-precond), not achieving the predicted 10–20× reader reduction. **MRA is the provisional cost/latency winner** across both briefs, pending quality comparison.

### The reframe

The reader is currently using the LLM as a *symbol index + dependency graph + retrieval engine + analyst*, all in one grinding pass, rebuilt from scratch on every commission. Only the "analyst" part genuinely needs a reasoning model. The first three are what non-LLM tooling does faster, cheaper, and more accurately.

**Why SSR failed**: it told the model to single-shot without preparing the context the model would need to actually single-shot. You can't single-shot over a raw codebase — the model has no way to answer "which files matter" except by exploring. The GSD pattern solves this by *pre-building the intel* and handing it to the consumer.

### GSD's model (from `docs/COMMANDS.md`)

GSD has three relevant commands:

- **`/gsd-map-codebase`** — spawns **four parallel mapper agents** for deep one-time analysis. Produces docs in `.planning/codebase/`. LLM-driven, expensive.
- **`/gsd-scan`** — single lightweight mapper agent, one focus area at a time (`tech`, `arch`, `quality`, `concerns`, `tech+arch`).
- **`/gsd-intel`** — the queryable intelligence layer. Stored as JSON at `.planning/intel/`: `stack`, `api-map`, `dependency-graph`, `file-roles`, `arch-decisions`. Operations: `status` (FRESH/STALE per file), `query <term>`, `diff` (changes since last snapshot), `refresh` (rebuild from analysis).

Key properties we want to steal:
- **Build once, query many.** The expensive analysis amortizes across downstream consumers.
- **Structural vs interpretive split.** Some intel is cheaply computable (dependency graph); some is semantic (file roles, arch decisions) and needs a model.
- **Freshness tracking.** FRESH/STALE per file, diff-since-last-snapshot. The scariest failure mode is trusting stale intel and planning against a codebase that moved.
- **Explicit refresh as an operation, not implicit per-consume.** Decouples the refresh decision from the query path.

### Three axes, not one (post-teardown framing)

The decomposition that settled this quest around a three-layer architecture came from pulling apart what I'd initially framed as "markdown vs JSON." There are actually *three* independent design axes, and the GSD dichotomy bundles them:

1. **Storage shape**: prose markdown vs structured JSON.
2. **Access shape**: preamble injection (blob loaded once) vs tool-call queries (on-demand).
3. **Production shape**: LLM-generated (interpretive, expensive) vs deterministically parsed (factual, cheap).

Each axis has its own tradeoffs and they're largely orthogonal. The quadrants matter:

- **Prose + preamble + LLM** — GSD's mature path. Good for interpretive context. Turn-killer. Cache-friendly. Our **layer 1**.
- **Structured + tool-call + deterministic** — facts exposed as cheap MCP tools. Good for on-demand fact-checks. Our **layer 2** (one of its two consumption paths).
- **Structured + preamble + deterministic** — facts rendered compactly into the preamble alongside prose, for commissions where exact-name precision matters. Our **layer 2** (the other consumption path).
- **Structured + query + LLM** — GSD's `/gsd-intel` quadrant. The quadrant we explicitly skip. LLM writes JSON unreliably, LLM reads JSON with weak attention priors, and query access reintroduces turns. GSD's own docs-vs-code drift in that layer is evidence that the shape fights itself.

The three-layer implementation plan follows directly from this axes analysis: layer 1 occupies the first quadrant, layer 2 occupies the second and third (two consumption paths on the same data), and layer 3 governs when the artifacts in those quadrants get rebuilt.

### Legacy layered view (pre-decomposition, kept for history)

| Layer | Build cost | Refresh cadence | Who builds | Contents |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Structural** | cheap | file change / git hook | tree-sitter, madge, scripts | file tree, symbols, dep graph, test-source map, recent git log |
| **Interpretive** | expensive (LLM) | staleness-triggered / on-demand | mapper pass (GSD-style) | file roles, arch decisions, module intents, convention notes |
| **Brief-specific** | cheap | per-commission | retrieval (embeddings/BM25) | candidate file set for *this* commission |

Astrolabe currently collapses all three into one grinding LLM pass, rebuilt from scratch every commission. GSD validates amortizing the middle layer. Embedding-based retrieval for the third layer is optional follow-on.

### Key design questions for later

- **Where does the intel live** — sanctum artifact, guild book, or engine-ephemeral? Each has different incremental-maintenance costs and coupling.
- **Consumer shape** — preamble injection (prompt stuff) vs MCP tools (agent queries)? Preamble maximizes cache reuse and minimizes turns; tools preserve agent autonomy and stay compatible with baseline/SSR/MRA alike.
- **Scope of "the codebase"** — just the framework repo (usual `draft.path` target), or also sanctum/vibers/domain? Reader currently scopes to the draft worktree.
- **Mapper as a rig** — the GSD-style one-time interpretive pass could itself be an Astrolabe-adjacent rig template. Refresh-as-a-commission.
- **Failure mode**: trusting stale intel. Need a freshness check before consumption, and a clear refresh trigger.
- **Order of operations**: finish the parent quest's A/B quality comparison first (that decides whether MRA is our target consumer or we're still optimizing a distinct reader stage).

### What we're *not* doing (yet)

- Not building embedding-based retrieval in the first cut. That's the brief-specific layer; it can come after the intel layer is proven.
- Not committing to replacing the reader entirely. If the atlas plus MRA holds quality, that's the endgame — but we gate on the A/B quality data.
- Not building our own parser infrastructure where an off-the-shelf tool (tree-sitter, madge, aider's repo-map) does the job. Vendor > build where it fits.

## References

- **Parent quest**: `w-mnt3t5h8-943e2a2ef85f` — Astrolabe efficiency: reader/full-inventory cost reduction.
- **Subquest layer 1**: `w-mnz8kj86-141e5b12bf7c` — Prose preamble generated by LLM mapper. Ships first.
- **Subquest layer 2**: `w-mnz8kqb9-266e6bb9f95c` — Deterministic repository facts. Ships second.
- **Subquest layer 3**: `w-mnz8kxki-bbe3be09d408` — Combatting staleness. Ships last; design work in parallel with 1+2 due to multi-rig coupling.
- **Multi-rig coupling**: `w-mnsxawnd-5f868b6491d9` (rig template generation), `w-mnsxbn8m-b3b9c9bc7c17` (cross-rig data flow and workspace persistence). Layer 3 coordinates with both.
- **Profile doc**: `experiments/data/2026-04-10-astrolabe-profile.md` — 51-session cohort, reader-cost analysis, intervention ranking, downstream-reuse finding.
- **GSD teardown**: `experiments/data/2026-04-14-gsd-intel-teardown.md` — full notes on GSD's `/gsd-map-codebase` + `/gsd-intel` systems, including templates, operations, orchestration, and lessons for our atlas.
- **GSD repo**: `https://github.com/gsd-build/get-shit-done` — source for intel schema, refresh logic, and mapper prompts.
- **GSD commands doc**: `https://github.com/gsd-build/get-shit-done/blob/main/docs/COMMANDS.md` — already surfaced `/gsd-map-codebase`, `/gsd-scan`, `/gsd-intel`.
- **Astrolabe source**: `/workspace/nexus/packages/plugins/astrolabe/src/astrolabe.ts` — `planningTemplate`, rig template mappings. Reader stage at lines 66–79; analyst at 88–102.
- **Astrolabe engines**: `/workspace/nexus/packages/plugins/astrolabe/src/engines/` — inventory-check, plan-init, decision-review, spec-publish.
- **Related: aider's repo-map** — tree-sitter-based symbol index with reference-weighted ranking. Prior art for the structural layer.
- **Related quest**: `w-mnt3r8al` — Engine completion predicated on tool-call trace. Orthogonal but relevant if reader becomes a query consumer (verifying the consume actually happened).
- **Related quest**: *Unified instrument context for cache efficiency* — shared-prefix caching trick on the instrument side; may generalize to atlas preamble injection.

## Notes

- (2026-04-14, Sean) Pointed specifically at `/gsd-map-codebase` as the inspiration, then confirmed interest in the `/gsd-intel` queryable-intelligence angle after Coco surfaced it as the better-fitting GSD primitive. Sean's framing: "maintain metadata about the project's shape in a way that can be consumed by the astrolabe without having to open/read all files or stuff a ton of content into the context."
- (2026-04-14) Three questions Sean explicitly asked this quest to answer: (1) what is the actual purpose of the reader step? (2) what is it providing to the analyst? (3) can any of that be generated or primed by a non-LLM task, and/or calculated+cached+incrementally modified?
  - Tentative answers from the conversation: (1) brief-specific code localization + risk surfacing, not generic project-shape extraction; (2) not much, empirically — analyst re-reads source directly and only saves ~27% via shared-conversation cache; (3) yes, substantially — structural intel is entirely non-LLM-friendly, interpretive intel amortizes across commissions via GSD-style one-time mapping.
- (2026-04-14) Dependency: the parent quest's A/B quality comparison needs to finish before we pick the consumer shape. If MRA holds quality, the atlas feeds MRA and we retire reader; if not, we're still tuning reader and the atlas feeds reader instead.
- (2026-04-14) **Post-teardown headline insight**: "Plan-phase already does the thing SSR failed at. It gets structured context about the codebase without the planning model doing its own exploration. The difference is that the context is *pre-built and document-subsetted*, not *generated inline*." This reframes our SSR failure as "single-shot without prep can't work" rather than "single-shot is fundamentally wrong" — the atlas is the prep step SSR was missing.
- (2026-04-14) **Key pattern to steal**: GSD's plan-phase uses a phase-type → document-subset map (e.g. `UI → CONVENTIONS.md + STRUCTURE.md`, `refactor → CONCERNS.md + ARCHITECTURE.md`) to pick which intel docs to load per commission. That's the "brief-shape-aware selective loading" primitive Astrolabe is missing and is the single most directly actionable port from GSD.
- (2026-04-14) **Drift warning from GSD**: the `/gsd-intel` refresh prompt names five JSON files (`stack`, `api-map`, `dependency-graph`, `file-roles`, `arch-decisions`) while the validator expects five different ones (`stack`, `apis`, `deps`, `files`, `arch.md`). Lesson: whatever schema we commit to, the mapper prompt, validator, consumer loaders, and docs have to share a single source of truth. Candidate: pin the schema in the framework ontology or `domain/`.
- (2026-04-14) **Three-layer decomposition** — Sean asked for the implementation work to be spun out as three subquests: (1) prose preamble generated by LLM, (2) deterministic repository facts, (3) combatting staleness, in that implementation order. Created as `w-mnz8kj86`, `w-mnz8kqb9`, `w-mnz8kxki`. This parent quest's role is now scaffolding + cross-cutting design coordination rather than direct implementation. Each layer owns its own detail.
- (2026-04-14) **Layer 3 ↔ multi-rig coupling** — Sean's framing: "a fresh map for XXX could be a declared dependency of an engine which could automatically trigger a prepend of the necessary planning engines." That's a concrete use case for the backward-chained precondition primitive being explored in `w-mnsxawnd` (Rig template generation) and has lifetime implications tied to `w-mnsxbn8m` (Cross-rig data flow and workspace persistence). Layer 3's design engages with both those quests rather than reinventing the mechanism inside this cluster.
- (2026-04-14) **Retrieval question raised on layer 1** — the BM25/vector-db/embedding question is captured on layer 1's quest (`w-mnz8kj86`) as an explicit open question with five options ranging from "no retrieval, static map only" through "hybrid map+BM25 fallback" to "embedding index." My current lean is option 1 (static map only for v1, amend the map when it's wrong), but the decision is parked for a real conversation once layer 1 is ready to commit.

parent.title: Astrolabe agility: reduce per-commission time and cost

items.title: Atlas layer 1: prose preamble generated by LLM mapper
